column2: Equivelar toroids with few flag-orbits
column3: An (n+1)-toroid is a quotient of a tessellation of the n-dimensional euclidean space with a lattice group. Toroids are generalizations of maps in the torus on higher dimensions and also provide examples of abstract polytopes. Equivelar maps in the 2-torus (3-toroids) where classified by Brehem and Kühnel in 2008. In 2012 Hubard, Orbanić, Pellicer and Weiss classified equivelar 4-toroids. However, a complete classification for any dimension seems to be a very hard problem. In the talk we will present a classification of equivelar (n+1)-toroids with less than n flag-orbits; in particular, we will discuss a classification of 2-orbit toroids of arbitrary dimension.
column7: Conference “Symmetries and Covers of Discrete Objects” (SCDO)

column2: Cubos generalizados y extensiones de politopos abstractos (Generalised cubes and extensions of abstract polytopes)
column3: La palabra "politopo" es el término genérico para los polígonos, poliedros, etc. Los politopos abstractos generalizan de manera combinatoria a los politopos clásicos. El problema de extensión en politopos convexos consiste en determinar cuúndo un politopo de dimensión d se puede usar para construir un politopo de dimensión d+1. Este problema tiene sentido en el contexto de los politopos abstractos. En la plática veremos la importancia de este problema y ofreceremos una posible solución que generaliza una construcción clásica de los cubos.
column7: 49th National Conference of the Mexican Mathematical Society

column2: Extensiones de politopos abstractos altamente simétricos   (Chiral extensions of highly symmetric polytopes)
column3: La palabra politopo es el término genérico de la sucesión punto, segmento, polígono, poliedro... Los politopos abstractos son generalizaciones combinatorias de los politopos convexos. Un problema clásico en la teoría de politopos convexos es el siguiente: dado un politopo d-dimensional K ¿existe un politopo (d + 1)-dimensional P tal que todas las caras d-dimensionales de P son isomorfas a K? En la plática mostraremos la situación de este problema en el contexto de politopos abstractos cuando imponemos algunas restricciones de simetría en K y en P.
column7: 2nd national meeting of graduate students of mathematics
column15: Unidad Cuernavaca del Instituto de Matemáticas, UNAM

column2: Chiral extensions of chiral polytopes
column3: Abstract polytopes are objects that generalize convex polytopes on a combinatorial way. An elementary problem in convex polytopes is, given an n-polytope K, is there an (n + 1)-polytope P such that all the facets of P are isomorphic to K? When this problem is presented in the context of abstract polytopes it becomes interesting when symmetry conditions are imposed over P (and hence, over K). In the talk we will explain the problem with detail when both P and K are chiral polytopes. In particular we will give some results regarding the possibilities for the Schläfli type of P and some examples for small values of n.
column7: 3rd Conference of the Pacific Rim Mathematical Association (PRIMA)

column2: Toros envolviendo esferas y otras aberraciones similares (Tori wrapping spheres and other similar aberrations)
column7: Aquelarre Matemático 2016

column2: Extensiones quirales de toros (Chiral extensions of toroids)
column3: Un poliedro puede ser pensado como un montón de polígonos que se pegan unos con otros a través de sus aristas. Esta noción es puramente combinatoria y se extiende de manera natural a dimensiones superiores de manera recursiva, es decir, podemos pensar a los polígonos como objetos 2-dimensionales y para n > 3, hablar de objetos n-dimensionales construidos a partir de objetos (n - 1)- dimensionales. Este problema se vuelve retador cuando ciertas condiciones de simetría son impuestas. En la plática exploraremos esta situación y hablaremos brevemente sobre una construcción de este estilo para ciertas teselaciones del toro.
column7: 51th National Conference of the Mexican Mathematical Society

column2: Highly symmetric toroidal polytopes
column3: In the talk we will review a technique that has been used to classify several kinds of highly symmetric abstract polytopes lying on a toroidal ambient space. We will focus not only on the abstract approach of the classifications but on visualizing such objects.
column7: 9th Slovenian Conference on Graph Theory (SiCGT)

column2: Chiral extensions of regular toroids
column3: An abstract polytope K is an extension of an abstract polytope P if all the facets of K are isomorphic to P. Chiral polytopes are those that have all possible rotations but  lack of reflectional symmetry. In the talk we will explore the problem of building chiral extensions of polytopes. We will also show a technique involving voltages over certain graphs to construct chiral extensions of regular toroids.
column7: 8th PhD Summer School in Discrete Mathematics

column2: De un ejemplo con gráficas a un problema con toros (From an example with graphs to a problem on tori)
column3: En esta plática exploraremos con detalle una estructura de incidencia sobre \(K_{6}\). Esta construcción nos servirá para motivar un problema general de mapas con cuadrados en el toro. Finalmente, mostraremos algunos resultados relativos a este problema.

column2: Extensiones de politopos áltamente simétricos (Extensions of highly symmetric polytopes)
column3: Un cubo puede ser pensado como una familia de cuadrados pegados por sus aristas. De manera similar, la teselación de cubos del espacio puede ser pensada como una familia de cubos pegados por sus caras cuadradas. Esta idea recursiva de construcción se puede entender no solo en el contexto de objetos geométricos como poliedros o teselaciones sino también en un contexto puramente combinatorio: el de los politopos abstractos. En la charla exploraremos una serie de problemas ligados a la pregunta: dado un politopo K de rango (dimensión) n, ¿Existe un politopo P de rango n+1 tal que todas las caras de rango n de P son isomorfas a K? De particular interés será cuando ponemos condiciones preestablecidas de simetría sobre P.
column7: Colloquium of the Institute of Mathematics UNAM (online)

column2: No me amenaces... no me amenaces (con que ya son todos)
column3: Existen exactamente 5 poliedros regulares: los Sólidos Platónicos. Para muchos de nosotros éste es uno de los teoremas que más pronto aprendimos en nuestra formación y, con un poco de suerte, hasta lo demostramos. Es más, este teorema constituye el clímax del último libro de Los Elementos de Euclides... pero... ¿Sí?... En la platica vamos a analizar un poquito este famoso teorema y sobre todo, platicaremos algo de historia al respecto.
column7: Math seminar FisMat UMSNH
column15: Faculty of Physics and Mathematics, UMSNH

column2: Highly symmetric polytopes with prescribed local combinatorics
column3: Convex polyhedra and tilings of the Euclidean of hyperbolic plane can be thought as a family of polygons glued together along their edges. This idea extends to higher dimensions: a 4-dimensional cube can be thought as family of 3-dimensional cubes glued together along its 2-dimensional faces. This way of thinking polytopes gives rise to some natural questions: given a n-dimensional polytope K, can we build a (n+1)-dimensional polytope P such that all the facets (maximal proper faces of P) are isomorphic to K? how many of such P can we build? Usually the geometry plays an important role on those questions.In the talk we will explore variants of this question in the context of abstract polytopes, where the geometry is less relevant. In particular, we are interested in the situation where prescribed symmetry conditions are imposed on P.
column7: Algebraic graph theory international webminar (online)

column2: Cayley extensions of maniplexes and polytopes
column3: A map on a surface whose automorphism group has a subgroup acting regularly on its vertices is called a Cayley map. Cayley extensions are a generalisation of that notion to maniplexes and polytopes. We define M to be a Cayley extension of K if the facets of M are isomorphic to K and if some subgroup of the automorphism group of M acts regularly on the facets of M. We will show that many natural extensions in the literature on maniplexes and polytopes are in fact Cayley extensions. We will also describe several universal Cayley extensions and examine the possibilities for their symmetry type graph.
column7: 10th Slovenian Conference on Graph Theory (SiCGT)

column2: A family of regular hypertopes built from regular polytopes
column3: A regular hypertope is a combinatorial object that generalises both regular hypermaps and regular polytopes. In this talk we will present a construction of a family of regular hypertopes with some preassigned conditions on their local combinatorics. This family is built from a family of regular polytopes.
column7: CMS Winter meeting 2019

column2: Voltage operations on maniplexes, maps and polytopes
column3: Geometrical operations such as truncation, duality, Petrie-duality, among others, arise naturally in the study of highly symmetric convex polyhedra. Often these operations do not generalise in an obvious way to combinatorial objects (such as abstract polytopes). In the talk we shall present a way to deal with many of these operations in a graph-theoretical setting using maniplexes, which are combinatorial generalisation of maps on surfaces and convex polytopes. We shall see how we could potentially use these operations to build maniplexes (or abstract polytopes) with prescribed symmetry type.ˀ
column7: Seminar Dicrete Mathematics UL FMF
column15: Faculty of Mathematics and Physics, University of Ljubljana

column2: Voltage operations on maniplexes, maps and polytopes
column3: Geometrical operations such as truncation, duality, Petrie-duality, among others, arise naturally in the study of highly symmetric convex polyhedra. Often these operations do not generalise in an obvious way to combinatorial objects (such as abstract polytopes). column2: Vertex trantisive graph with (very) small movement
column3: The motion of a graph is defined as the smallest number of vertices that are not fixed by a non-trivial automorphism. In the talk we will show a classification of the vertex transitive graphs whose motion is a prime number or a (very)small integer. We shall also explore how this problem connects with some classical permutation groups and graph theory problems.
column7: Conference “Symmetries of Discrete Objects” (SODO)

column2: Symmetries of voltage operations on maniplexes and polytopes
column3: A classical result in the theory of maps on surfaces states that the medial of a regular map has two orbits on flags (e.g., it has two types of faces). If the original map is self-dual, however, its medial is regular — it admits symmetry that is somewhat unexpected. This prompts a natural analogue for truncation: what is the symmetry-type of the truncation of a given (say, regular) map? What conditions on the original map guarantee or forbid the emergence of unexpected symmetries for its truncation? Although the answer is known, it is less intuitive than in the medial case. In joint work with Hubard and Mochán, we introduced the notion of voltage operations, which generalise many classical operations on maps and polyhedra to the broader setting of maniplexes and abstract polytopes. In this talk, I will revisit voltage operations and present some results and ideas concerning the central question: when does an operation yield a result with unexpected symmetry?
column7: Workshop “Graphs Embeddings and Maps on Surfaces” (GEMS)

column2: Are your polyhedra the same as my polyhedra?
column3: SLO: Ali veste, kaj je pravilni polieder? Ste ga kdaj opazili na pločniku? Veste, koliko jih sploh obstaja? Na predavanju bo predavatelj povedal zgodbo, ki se začne v stari Grčiji in se razteza vse do trenutnega stoletja. Ta zgodba se vrti okoli zgornjih vprašanj in o tem, kako so se odgovori nanja skozi različna zgodovinska obdobja spreminjali.ENG: Do you know what a regular polyhedron is? Have you ever seen one on the sidewalk? Do you know how many of them there are? In the talk, I will tell you a story, a story that starts in ancient Greece and goes all the way to this century. This story goes around the questions above and how they have had different answers across different periods of time.
column7: Cycle “Poljudnih predavanj iz matematike,fizike in  astronomije” (Popular lectures in mathematics, physics and astronomy)
